1.           Communication 
a.           Internal Communication - IRT team
Microsoft Teams will serve as the primary platform for coordinating responses to cybersecurity incidents at Ahlers Logistics. For incidents classified as critical or high severity, a dedicated Microsoft Teams chat group will be established, including relevant personnel with roles assigned at that time. This group will facilitate real-time communication, including chat, calls, and secure sharing of incident-related information.
In cases where Microsoft Teams or email accounts are suspected of being compromised, unavailable, or their use could alert an adversary, alternative communication methods may be employed. A temporary WhatsApp group may be created for chat or voice communication; however, no incident-related documentation, information, or data may be shared via WhatsApp to maintain security and confidentiality.
b.           Internal Communication - Employees / Board
Effective internal communication during a cybersecurity incident ensures that employees and the board are informed, aligned, and equipped to support response efforts.
Employees
·       Who to contact: All employees should be notified by the communication officer or IRT lead. In case of any concerns, employees can reach out to helpdesk@ahlers.com (or security@ahlers.com , TBD)
·       When to contact: High or critical severity cybersecurity incidents must be communicated within four hours of incident confirmation. Ongoing updates will be provided daily to ensure all employees remain informed. 
·       How to contact: Communication will happen through email (preferred) or Microsoft Teams. 
Board
·       Who to contact: The board should be notified by the communication officer or IRT lead. 
·       When to contact: High or critical severity cybersecurity incidents must be communicated to relevant stakeholders within four hours of confirmation. Ongoing updates will be provided daily to ensure the board remain informed of response progress and developments. Upon resolution, a comprehensive post-incident report will be presented to the board, detailing the incident’s outcome, impact, and lessons learned to inform future preparedness.
·       How to contact: Communication will happen through email (preferred) or Microsoft Teams. 
c.           External Communication – Customers / Authorities / other stakeholders
In the event of a cybersecurity incident, external communication with customers, authorities, or other stakeholders may be necessary. Prior to any external communication, the Incident Response Team (IRT) must consult the following stakeholders for guidance and approval:
·       Legal Counsel: Ensures compliance with applicable legal and regulatory requirements and advises on the content and timing of notifications. This can be either Ahlers Logistics’ legal counsel or an external organisation involved in the investigation and follow-up of the incident. (e.g. through AIG)
·       Communications Officer: Develops and reviews communication materials to ensure clarity, accuracy, and alignment with Ahlers Logistics’ messaging standards.
·       Executive Leadership: Approves all external communications to ensure strategic alignment and organizational representation.
·       Business Unit Leader / Sales Rep: Business unit leaders and sales representatives must be engaged when customer operations or services are disrupted, ensuring alignment with client-specific service-level agreements (SLAs) and recovery expectations.

1.           Incident Response Handling Procedure
1.1                 Detection & Notification
The Security Operations Center (SOC) service continuously monitors all systems, networks, and data managed by Ahlers IT to detect malicious activity or behavior. Upon identifying suspicious activity, the SOC will automatically issue a notification. Employees who observe suspicious behavior may manually report it for further analysis by the designated personnel.
a.           Automatic Detection & Notification 
Upon detecting a cybersecurity incident, the Security Operations Center (SOC) will notify the cybersecurity manager and/or IT operations team, based on the incident’s severity and detection time, via email, SMS, or phone call, as appropriate. Receipt of this notification initiates the incident handling procedure. No manual intervention is needed in this process. 
Incident escalation / notification path: 
·       Detected & prevented, blocked, sinkholed: mail 
·       Detected & not prevented, blocked, sinkholed: mail & SMS
·       Detected & not prevented, blocked, sinkholed & time between 6pm – 8am: mail & SMS & call (up to 3 times)
 
Mail: xsoarmail@ahlers.com
SMS: +32 472 33 53 91 (Cybersecurity Manager)
Call: +32 472 33 53 91 (Cybersecurity Manager) from number:  +32460203387
 
b.           Manual Detection & Notification
When employees identify abnormal or suspicious activity (incl phishing), they have the obligation to contact the local helpdesk to initiate an initial investigation. If the helpdesk determines that the activity is likely a cybersecurity-related incident, they will escalate the matter to the Ahlers IT cybersecurity manager or IT operations for further investigation.

When additional expertise is required to assess the validity, impact, or severity of a cybersecurity incident, it is recommended to contact our SOC service provider. They can be reached at soc@jarviss.be or +32 78 31 99 88 for specialized support in incident analysis and response.

At Ahlers Logistics, we utilize several technological mechanisms for the automated detection of cybersecurity incidents. In addition to these automated systems, our personnel are trained to detect incidents and notify the necessary IT staff promptly.

Classification
Upon detection or reporting of a cybersecurity incident, the following steps must be executed to assess and address the incident effectively:
1.           Investigation: Conduct an initial investigation to determine the incident’s scope, impact, and affected systems.
2.           Classification: Categorize the incident’s severity based on the predefined criteria below. 
3.           Response Initiation: Respond with the appropriate actions.
a.          Investigation
Upon detection or reporting of a cybersecurity incident, an initial investigation must be conducted to assess the situation, scope, and organizational impact. The following steps will guide the process:
·       Analyze the Incident: Gather details about the incident, including date, time, source, and observed effects, to understand its nature and origin.[BH1] 
·       Determine the Scope: Identify which users, systems, networks, and/or data are affected. 
·       Evaluate Organizational Impact: Assess the potential consequences for the organization. 
Once the initial investigation is complete, proceed to classify the criticality of the incident.
b.          Classification
Upon completion of the initial investigation, the collected data will be used to classify the incident’s criticality through the following steps:
·       Identify Affected Systems: Determine which systems, networks, or data managed by Ahlers IT are impacted, including on-premises infrastructure, cloud environments, or third-party services under its oversight.
 
·       Assess Organizational Impact: Evaluate the incident’s effects on:
·       Business operations
·       Finances
·       Organizational reputation
·       Legal 
·       Determine Criticality: Classify the incident as informational, low, medium, high, or critical severity based on predefined criteria.
Upon completion of the incident criticality classification, the appropriate incident handling process can be initiated.
Identify Affected System (s)
Understanding which systems are affected by a cybersecurity incident and their business criticality to Ahlers Logistics is essential for an effective response. The following 3 classifications are used: 
Scope
Systems
Critical Systems
·       Transport Management Systems - Alaska
·       Warehouse Management Systems
·       IT systems / Infra (Entra ID / DNS / Domain Controller)
·       Microsoft 365 environment
Important Systems
·       HR systems 
·       Financial Systems
Non-Critical Systems
·       End-user devices
·       IoT
·       Other…
 
Assess Organizational Impact
Assessing the organizational impact of a cybersecurity incident is critical for an effective response. Accurate judgment in the following four key areas is essential to guide incident handling:
Impact
Financial
Business Operations
Reputational
Legal / Regulatory
No Impact
No financial impact
No impact on business operations
No impact on customer relationships
No legal or regulatory breaches. No governmental institutions involved. No risk of claims.
Minimal Impact
<10.000 EUR
Full halt of the business operations for one person
Impact on the relationship of one non-key account
Potential data leakage of non-sensitive data. No governmental institutions involved. No risk of legal claims by customers
Moderate Impact
<50.000 EUR
Full halt of the business operations for one team
Impact on the relationship with multiple non-key accounts
Data leakage of non-sensitive data. No governmental institutions involved. Some risk of legal claims by customers
Significant Impact
<100.000 EUR
Full halt of the business operations for one department
Impact on the relationship with one key account
Data leakage of sensitive PII. GBA involved. High probability of legal claims by customers
Major Impact
>100.000 EUR
Full halt of the business operations for the whole company
Impact on the relationship with multiple key accounts
Multiple instances of government involved (GBA, Cybercrime unit, etc.). Certainty of legal claims by customers.
 
Determine Criticality
The criticality of a cybersecurity incident is determined by evaluating the business criticality of affected systems and the anticipated organizational impact:
Scope/Severity
No Impact
Minimal Impact
Moderate Impact
Significant Impact
Major Impact
Critical Systems
Low
High
High
Critical
Critical
Important Systems
Low
Medium
High
High
Critical
Non-critical Systems
Informational*
Low
Medium
High
High
*Incidents classified as “Informational” do not have a documented follow-up process.

·       Root Cause Analysis: Conduct a detailed investigation to identify the incident’s cause, scope, and impact on systems, networks, or data managed by Ahlers Logistics. This analysis will assess the effectiveness of response actions and identify vulnerabilities or gaps.

Detailed documentation of all findings and actions taken during the detection and analysis phase is crucial. This information serves as a valuable record for future reference and aids in ensuring a systematic and organized approach to incident management.
